_comment: Page-1 classification. Replaces routing on the filename.
_why: The folder is authoritative for WHO the patient is. It says nothing about WHAT the document is -- a lab report sits under a specialty folder, a prescription is titled with the doctor's name. Keyword-matching the title was a shortcut, and it silently skipped hundreds of documents. The document itself says what it is; read it. This also works for documents that arrive with no filename at all (forwarded into a chat, say).
_cost: One call per document, page 1 only. Cheap next to a full extraction, and it stops us extracting a lab prompt against an insurance policy.
prompt:
You are classifying a medical document. Look at it and answer what KIND of document it is.

Return a JSON object with exactly these keys:

- "doc_type": exactly one of:
    "lab"          - laboratory test results (blood, urine, culture, biopsy report)
    "prescription" - an outpatient consultation note or prescription
    "discharge"    - a hospital discharge summary / inpatient case sheet
    "radiology"    - an imaging report (X-ray, USG, CT, MRI, echo, mammogram)
    "vaccination"  - a vaccination record or certificate
    "insurance"    - an insurance policy, claim, or medical bill
    "other"        - anything else (an ID card, an image with no report, a form)

- "confidence": "high" | "low". Use "low" if you are unsure, if the page is unreadable, or if it could plausibly be two of these. Do not guess confidently.

- "has_medications": true if the document lists medicines the patient is to take, false otherwise. A discharge summary and a prescription usually do; a lab report does not.

- "reason": a short phrase quoting what on the page told you (e.g. "header says OUTPATIENT RECORD", "contains a table of test results with reference ranges").

Answer only from what is on the page. If the page is blank or unreadable, return doc_type "other" with confidence "low".
